content:
5 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

You bump the parent POM to Spring Boot 4, run mvn verify, and the build is green. Faster than usual, even. Then you look at the test count and it dropped from 812 to 517.
Nothing failed. Nothing errored. Three hundred JUnit 4 tests simply stopped being discovered.
If you are coming from Spring Boot 3, you are used to spring-boot-starter-test quietly carrying junit-vintage-engine around for you, so that the @RunWith(SpringRunner.class) tests written in 2019 kept running next to the Jupiter ones. Spring Boot 4 (released November 2025, on Spring Framework 7) stops doing that. No vintage engine on the classpath means no engine claims your JUnit 4 classes, and the JUnit Platform does not consider “I found a class nobody wants to run” an error.
This is the part of the migration that bites quietly. Here is how to find it and fix it.
First: prove how many tests you actually lost
Before changing anything, record the baseline on your current branch.
# on the Spring Boot 3 branchmvn -q clean verify
grep -rh "Tests run:" target/surefire-reports/*.txt \
| awk -F'[ ,]' '{s+=$3} END {print "total:", s}'
